one_line_takeaway: A preliminary unsupervised RAG defense shows that generator activations, output embeddings, and entropy-based TokenSAR uncertainty -- computed even from a generic context-summary prompt that hides the attacker's target question -- can jointly flag about 80% of PoisonedRAG-style adversarial contexts via simple statistical outlier detection, though ~19.5% of attacks still evade detection and false-positive behavior remains unquantified.
key_finding: Table I (paper's own results, single experimental run on HotpotQA+PoisonedRAG) reports, out of the AC-containing context combinations for both prompt types: Invalid = 11, Undetected = 486, detected by TokenSAR+Embeddings only = 60 (summary) / 60 (question), by TokenSAR+Activations only = 38 (summary) / 22 (question), by TokenSAR+Embeddings+Activations jointly = 1912 (summary) / 1928 (question). Per-indicator totals: TokenSAR total = 2010 (both prompts identical), Embeddings total = 1972 (summary) / 1988 (question), Activations total = 1950 (summary) / 1950 (question). Overall, about 19.5% of attacks (486 of 2,496 AC-containing combinations) went undetected for both prompt types. TokenSAR had the highest individual detection count, and every detected attack was caught by at least two of the three indicators, with most caught by all three, indicating high redundancy but some complementarity among indicators. Critically, the summary prompt (which withholds the target question from the detector) performed essentially on par with the question prompt across all indicators, supporting the paper's claim that ACD does not require knowledge of the attacker's target prompt.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a preliminary, unsupervised Adversarial Context Detector (ACD) for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The authors propose detecting adversarial (poisoned) context documents at inference time using an elementary statistical outlier test (Grubb's test, alpha=0.1) applied to three candidate indicator quantities computed from the generator LLM's response to a context set: (1) the generator's last-layer activations, (2) 768-dimensional output embeddings of the generated text (using a variant of the MPNet model, all-mpnet-base-v2), and (3) TokenSAR, an entropy-based predictive-uncertainty score computed from output logits. A reference distribution for each quantity is built from N=10 combinations of known-valid (non-adversarial) contexts per question; a new combination is flagged as adversarial if its quantity value falls outside [mean - Gcrit, mean + Gcrit] of the reference distribution. The paper's second original claim is that the detector does not need the attacker's target question/prompt to work: instead of prompting the generator with the actual user question, they use a fixed generic 'summarize the following context documents' prompt, and show this summary-based detection is comparably effective to using the real question prompt.
threat_model: Attacker poisons the RAG context document database with Adversarial Contexts (ACs) rather than attacking the prompt directly, since document poisoning is persistent and affects all users of the system (e.g., an attacker emailing a poisoned document to an email-bot's context store, or seeding a web-search index). The defender does not know which specific question(s) the attacker is targeting and must detect the attack by screening contexts alone, before/without seeing the adversarial target prompt. The paper defines three assumed defender knowledge levels: (a) black-box query access to the RAG generator's text output only; (b) access to output logits (enabling TokenSAR); (c) access to the generator LLM's internal layer activations. The method is evaluated specifically against PoisonedRAG-style knowledge-corruption attacks (Zou et al., USENIX Security 2025), which is explicitly the paper's own experimental threat instantiation, not a general claim.
limitations: The authors explicitly and repeatedly describe this as a small, preliminary, work-in-progress feasibility study, not a mature detection system. Stated limitations/future work from the paper itself: (1) the study used a relatively small number of normal (non-adversarial) reference samples (N=10 per question), which the authors say is insufficient to properly quantify false-positive rates (valid contexts incorrectly flagged as adversarial) -- no false-positive rate is reported in this paper; (2) the approach is evaluated against only one attack family, PoisonedRAG, and the authors explicitly state it needs to be extended to other RAG attacks such as corpus poisoning, BadRAG, and GARAG; (3) the statistical method (Grubb's test) implicitly assumes normal distribution of the indicator quantities and the authors note they are 'aware of the interdependence' among the center-distance-based quantities, i.e., an acknowledged methodological approximation; (4) they characterize the outlier test as 'elementary' and state that 'more sophisticated anomaly detection methods shall be used' in future work, citing prior network intrusion detection research as inspiration; (5) activations require white-box/internal model access and 'might not be available, especially for proprietary models and APIs,' limiting real-world applicability of that particular indicator.

datasets_used: HotpotQA (100 sampled questions, multi-hop QA with Wikipedia contexts and gold answers)
PoisonedRAG adversarial-context dataset (targeted attack results for HotpotQA)
quantitative_results: 486 of 2,496 AC-containing context combinations (about 19.5%) undetected, for both summary and question prompts
TokenSAR total detections: 2010 (identical for summary and question prompts)
Embeddings total detections: 1972 (summary prompt) vs 1988 (question prompt)
Activations total detections: 1950 (summary prompt) vs 1950 (question prompt)
Detected by TokenSAR+Embeddings only: 60 (both prompt types)
Detected by TokenSAR+Activations only: 38 (summary prompt) vs 22 (question prompt)
Detected jointly by TokenSAR+Embeddings+Activations: 1912 (summary prompt) vs 1928 (question prompt)
11 of the sampled HotpotQA questions marked invalid (insufficient valid, correctly-answerable context combinations)
Up to 30 AC-containing context combinations per question (k=5 valid contexts, N=10 combinations, successively replaced by AC)
baselines_compared: No external supervised baseline detector is run for comparison; the paper instead compares its three unsupervised indicators (activations, embeddings, TokenSAR) against each other and across two prompting strategies (question prompt vs. summary prompt) as its internal ablation/comparison
